stories.headline: Update: Roommate Charged with Two Counts of First-Degree Murder in Deaths of USF Doctoral Students
stories.summary:
Hisham Abugharbieh, 26, has been charged with two counts of premeditated first-degree murder with a weapon in the deaths of University of South Florida doctoral students Zamil Limon and Nahida Bristy, the Hillsborough County Sheriff's Office announced Saturday. Abugharbieh was Limon's roommate and was arrested following a standoff with police earlier in the week after the two Bangladeshi students, both 27, were reported missing.

Authorities found Limon dead and, as of the announcement, continued searching for Bristy. The case drew national attention after the students' disappearance was reported earlier this week. Abugharbieh faces the most serious homicide charges available under Florida law.

The University of South Florida and the Bangladeshi-American community have expressed grief over the killings. The circumstances and motive behind the alleged murders have not been fully disclosed by authorities. Investigators have not said publicly whether Bristy has been located or what evidence led to the double murder charges.

stories.headline: NYC Mayor Mamdani Faces Community Pushback Over Police Cuts as Bronx Parents Plead for School Crossing Guard
stories.summary:
Parents at a Bronx elementary school are pressing New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani's administration for a police crossing guard at a dangerous intersection near Zeta Bronx Tremont Park Lower Elementary School, where they say near-accidents involving children occur regularly. More than 1,000 people have signed a Change.org petition supporting the request at the intersection of Arthur Avenue and Tremont Avenue, which funnels traffic directly onto Interstate 95.

The pleas come as Mamdani has moved to cut the NYPD budget and cancel approximately 5,000 planned new officer hires, a decision that has drawn criticism from public safety advocates and some residents who had previously supported the mayor. Mamdani's office did not respond to a request for comment from Fox News Digital.

The tension reflects a broader debate in New York City between the mayor's stated policing philosophy — which critics link to past support for defunding the police — and the on-the-ground safety concerns of constituents in outer-borough neighborhoods. Some Bronx parents interviewed said they had initially supported Mamdani but expressed growing concern about his approach. 'Removing or preventing us from having those resources is a step in the wrong direction when our schools and children clearly need more support,' said one parent in a statement. Supporters of Mamdani's approach argue that reducing over-policing and investing in alternative safety infrastructure produces better long-term community outcomes.

stories.headline: Trump Faces Mounting Political Headwinds as Economy Polling Shifts, Johnson Confronts Fractious House Week
stories.summary:
President Trump is heading into a politically difficult stretch after a week of setbacks, with new polling showing voters preferring Democrats on the economy for the first time since 2010 and Republican voters expressing disapproval of Trump's handling of inflation. The data compounds pressure on an administration already navigating diplomatic setbacks and intraparty friction.

House Speaker Mike Johnson faces a grueling pre-recess schedule next week, with three high-stakes votes on the agenda: a long-term extension of Section 702 FISA surveillance authorities, a farm bill, and a Senate-linked measure. Johnson has built a reputation for engineering narrow victories, but rank-and-file Republicans have shown increased willingness to defy Trump, whose intervention the speaker typically relies on to close difficult votes.

The economic polling shift is particularly notable given that Republican electoral advantages on the economy have persisted for more than a decade. Democrats argue the numbers reflect public concern over tariff-driven inflation and trade uncertainty. Trump allies contend the polling reflects short-term turbulence and that long-term economic fundamentals, including deregulation and energy policy, will improve voter sentiment before the 2026 midterms.

stories.headline: Patriots Coach Mike Vrabel Steps Away From Team Amid Affair Scandal at NFL Draft
stories.summary:
New England Patriots head coach Mike Vrabel departed his team's operations on Day 3 of the 2026 NFL Draft following the publication of photographs showing him kissing ESPN NFL reporter Dianna Russini, which emerged after weeks of swirling speculation. Vrabel held a press conference before the first round began, acknowledging that his 'previous actions don't meet the standard that I hold myself to,' before abruptly leaving the podium mid-questioning.

Vrabel had previously dismissed the photographs, published earlier in April by the New York Post from an Arizona resort, as 'laughable,' a statement he appeared to walk back at the press conference, framing the denial as 'an attempt to protect your family.' The Patriots announced his departure from draft activities early Thursday morning, with his long-term status with the team left unresolved.

The controversy overshadowed the Patriots' draft selections, including offensive tackle Caleb Lomu from Utah in the first round. The draft itself generated additional debate over the New York Giants' choice of Ohio State linebacker Arvell Reese at No. 5 overall, which surprised many analysts, and the Los Angeles Rams' surprise quarterback selection that drew widespread reaction.

stories.headline: Trump Cancels Envoys' Pakistan Trip as Iran Talks Collapse; Araghchi Leaves Islamabad
stories.summary:
President Trump canceled a planned trip by special envoy Steve Witkoff and son-in-law Jared Kushner to Islamabad on Saturday, hours after the White House had announced the delegation would travel there for Pakistani-brokered peace talks with Iran. Trump told reporters he saw "no point" in an 18-hour flight given the current state of negotiations, adding "we have all the cards" and that Iran could call the U.S. anytime. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi had arrived in Islamabad on Friday but departed before the U.S. envoys were set to arrive, with Tehran maintaining it would not meet directly with American officials.

The breakdown marks a significant setback in efforts to end the roughly two-month U.S.-Israel military campaign against Iran, which President Trump announced on February 28. The Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed to commercial shipping, disrupting global oil markets and supply chains. Germany announced it is sending naval units to the Mediterranean in preparation for a possible international mission in the strait, and California jet fuel stocks have dropped to levels not seen since 2023.

Iran said any observations it had would be conveyed to Pakistan as an intermediary, refusing direct engagement. Former National Security Adviser John Bolton and former Secretary of State Mike Pompeo both publicly expressed skepticism about the prospects for a deal before the talks fell apart. A Republican lawmaker said U.S.-Iranian talks are unlikely to make major progress in the near term.

Trump suggested future negotiations could take place by phone, posting on Truth Social that Iran need only "call!!" The cancellation leaves the ceasefire's status uncertain. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt had said earlier Friday that Vice President Vance remained on standby to travel to Pakistan if deemed necessary.

stories.headline: Orbán Renounces Parliamentary Seat After Landslide Election Defeat Ends 16-Year Rule
stories.summary:
Hungarian outgoing Prime Minister Viktor Orbán announced Saturday that he will not take his seat in parliament following his party Fidesz's landslide defeat in the April 12 elections, saying he is "needed not in parliament, but in the reorganisation of the patriotic movement." Fidesz was reduced from 135 seats to 52 in Hungary's 199-seat parliament, with opposition party Tisza — led by former Fidesz insider Péter Magyar — winning a supermajority of more than two-thirds.

Orbán, 62, has held a parliamentary seat since 1990 and led Fidesz throughout that period, serving as prime minister since 2010. He attributed his renunciation of the seat to the mandate belonging to the party rather than to him personally. Fidesz's parliamentary bloc will be led from Monday by Gulyás Gergely, who previously oversaw the prime minister's office. Orbán's future as Fidesz party leader will be decided at a conference in June.

Magyar has pledged to reverse Orbán-era changes to education and healthcare, restore judicial independence, and dismantle the patronage system known as NER that critics say enriched party loyalists. He has also pledged warmer ties with the European Union and Ukraine, a sharp departure from Orbán's alignment with U.S. President Trump and Russian President Putin. "Russians go home" was a frequent chant at Tisza campaign rallies.

Hungary's new parliament is scheduled to hold its first session on May 9. The transition represents one of the most significant political shifts in Central Europe in years, with implications for EU cohesion policy and the bloc's unified stance on the war in Ukraine.

stories.headline: Update: Lebanon-Israel Ceasefire Fraying on Ground Despite Three-Week Extension
stories.summary:
Even as Israel and Lebanon agreed this week to extend their ceasefire by three weeks — a deal announced following a Thursday Oval Office meeting — the truce is showing serious signs of collapse on the ground, with violations reported by both Israel and Hezbollah. PBS NewsHour correspondent Simona Foltyn reports from southern Lebanon, where villagers are returning to bury the dead amid ongoing tensions. At least 100 first responders, including ambulance workers, were killed by Israeli strikes during the preceding conflict, according to local accounts.

Israel killed more than 2,000 Lebanese during 45 days of fighting, Lebanese communities say. In the village of Itid, 18 people — including both civilians and Hezbollah fighters — were laid to rest following the ceasefire's entry into force. Residents interviewed expressed fierce opposition to their government's direct talks with Israel, which has demanded Hezbollah be disarmed as a condition for a lasting peace.

Hezbollah supporters in affected communities frame the group's armed presence as essential to their defense and dignity, with residents saying disarmament is not possible given what they describe as Israel's targeting of civilians and non-combatants. Israel has maintained that its strikes targeted Hezbollah military infrastructure and that the group bears responsibility for using civilian areas.

The fragility of the ceasefire carries implications beyond Lebanon, intersecting with the broader U.S.-Iran conflict and ongoing efforts to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Any renewed escalation between Israel and Hezbollah could further complicate the already stalled diplomatic track between Washington and Tehran.

stories.headline: Bellevue Hospital Reassigns Undocumented Immigrant Patients Amid Immigration Status Disclosure Fears
stories.summary:
Officials at Bellevue Hospital in New York City moved some undocumented immigrant patients to other areas of the facility after internal concerns arose over the potential disclosure of patients' immigration status, according to reporting published Saturday. Hospital administrators confirmed the reassignments, stating that affected patients would continue to receive care elsewhere within the institution.

The move reflects growing anxiety among public hospital officials nationwide about the legal and ethical obligations surrounding patient privacy and immigration enforcement. Bellevue, one of the country's oldest and largest public hospitals, serves a large immigrant population and has historically provided care regardless of immigration status.

Supporters of immigrant patients argue the reassignments risk creating a chilling effect that could deter vulnerable individuals from seeking necessary medical care. Critics of sanctuary-style hospital policies counter that institutions receiving federal funding must comply with applicable federal law, including cooperation with immigration authorities when legally required.

The situation highlights the ongoing tension between federal immigration enforcement priorities and local public health institutions that serve mixed-status communities, a conflict that has intensified in numerous cities since the beginning of the current administration.

stories.headline: Mexico Says Two US Agents Killed in Crash Were Unauthorized to Operate on Mexican Soil
stories.summary:
The Mexican government has concluded that two US agents — reportedly CIA operatives — who died in a car crash on April 19 had no formal authorization to conduct operational activities in Mexico. Following an investigation ordered by President Claudia Sheinbaum, Mexico's security ministry said neither agent held accreditation permitting participation in operations on Mexican territory, and that federal authorities had not been informed of their presence. Immigration records showed one entered as a tourist-category visitor while the other traveled on a diplomatic passport.

The agents were part of a convoy returning from a raid on suspected methamphetamine laboratories in a remote, mountainous area of Chihuahua state when their vehicle veered off a mountain road and plunged into a ravine. Two members of the Chihuahua State Investigation Agency also died in the crash. Chihuahua's state attorney general had described the US officials as 'instructor officers' from the US embassy engaged in training activities, a characterization that conflicts with the federal government's findings.

The incident sharpens existing tensions between Washington and Mexico City over counter-narcotics cooperation. Sheinbaum has consistently refused offers of US-led operations on Mexican soil, insisting on Mexican sovereignty while pledging continued intelligence-sharing. The Trump administration has pressed Mexico aggressively on drug trafficking, and a Reuters investigation last year found the CIA had been running covert operations in Mexico for years with the approval of prior Mexican administrations.

The dual deaths raise questions about the scope of US unilateral operations in Mexico and whether they proceeded without Sheinbaum's knowledge or consent. Mexico's position — that no joint operations were underway — directly contradicts accounts suggesting routine operational collaboration. The diplomatic fallout from the episode is expected to complicate ongoing US-Mexico security negotiations.

stories.headline: ICE Arrests Drop Nearly 12% in Weeks Following Minneapolis Killings of Two American Citizens
stories.summary:
Immigration and Customs Enforcement arrests fell by nearly 12 percent in the weeks following the killings of two American citizens — Renee Good and Alex Pretti — by immigration officers in Minneapolis in late January, according to an Associated Press analysis of federal data provided to UC Berkeley's Deportation Data Project. Weekly arrests averaged 7,369 nationwide in the five weeks after Border Czar Tom Homan announced a drawdown of agents in Minnesota, down from 8,347 per week in the prior five weeks. Those figures remain dramatically higher than enforcement levels during the Biden administration.

The Minneapolis killings triggered a broader shake-up of immigration leadership. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem was fired in early March, and Border Patrol commander Gregory Bovino — the public face of the aggressive 'turn and burn' enforcement strategy — was pushed aside. Arrests had peaked at nearly 40,000 in December before the controversy, with officers conducting raids at restaurant kitchens, bus stops, and retail parking lots across major cities.

The decline was not uniform nationally. Arrests rose sharply in Kentucky, Indiana, North Carolina, and Florida during the same period, with Kentucky's weekly arrest count more than doubling. Large states including Texas and Minnesota saw steep drops. The Trump administration has maintained that its operations target dangerous criminals living in the country illegally, with the president describing those arrested as 'the worst of the worst,' though the AP noted the composition of arrestees is more varied than that characterization suggests.

Polling has found broad public concern that the Minneapolis operation exceeded appropriate bounds, a sentiment that likely contributed to the leadership changes. Supporters of aggressive enforcement argue the overall arrest numbers remain historically elevated and reflect a necessary correction after what they describe as years of lax border enforcement under the prior administration. Critics contend the Minneapolis deaths and subsequent policy retreat illustrate the dangers of prioritizing enforcement theatrics over due process.

stories.headline: Venezuela's Interim Government Moves to End Political Prisoner Release Scheme After Nine Weeks, Drawing Sharp Criticism
stories.summary:
Interim Venezuelan President Delcy Rodríguez declared that a scheme to release political prisoners was 'coming to an end' after just nine weeks in operation, drawing immediate condemnation from human rights organizations. Prisoner rights group Foro Penal said approximately 473 people it classifies as political prisoners had been freed under the amnesty law passed by Venezuela's National Assembly, while more than 500 are believed to remain incarcerated. The National Assembly had initially indicated the law would eventually cover 11,000 people.

Rodríguez, speaking at a meeting of justice officials in Caracas on Friday, credited the amnesty program as 'very successful' and said 8,616 people had been freed under it — a figure significantly larger than Foro Penal's count of those it classifies as political prisoners, suggesting the government is counting a broader population of detainees. Rights groups said Rodríguez lacked the legal authority to terminate the releases, with Foro Penal's vice-president Gonzalo Himiob stating that only a new legislative act or referendum could undo the amnesty law, which carries no expiration date.

Watchdog organization Provea called the move 'arbitrary and unconstitutional,' while the Justice, Encounter and Pardon group described it as 'a grave assault on the rule of law,' saying the amnesty had proven 'an exercise in political rhetoric rather than a genuine instrument to restore freedom.' The release of political prisoners — including colleagues of opposition leader María Corina Machado — had been a key condition attached to US support for Rodríguez's interim administration following the US seizure of former President Nicolás Maduro in January.

The development puts the interim government in a delicate position with Washington. President Trump had backed Rodríguez over Machado, and the prisoner releases were understood as a central concession to secure that backing. Whether the US will press Rodríguez to reverse course or treat the episode as an internal Venezuelan matter is unclear, but the episode illustrates the fragility of political arrangements in the country following Maduro's removal.

stories.headline: U.S. Munitions Stocks Under Strain After Iran Campaign, Analysts Warn of Long-Term Supply Risk
stories.summary:
Despite President Trump's public assertions that the United States has a "virtually unlimited supply" of key munitions and could fight wars "forever," congressional testimony and independent analysis paint a more constrained picture. A Center for Strategic and International Studies report concluded the U.S. may have expended more than half of its prewar inventory of at least four key munitions, including Tomahawk cruise missiles, during the five-week bombing campaign against Iran.

Adm. Samuel Paparo, head of U.S. Indo-Pacific Command, told the Senate Armed Services Committee this week that scaling production of high-end systems such as the Tomahawk or the AGM-158 JASSM could take one to two years for manufacturers like Lockheed Martin and Raytheon. "There are finite limits to the magazine," Paparo said, adding that he has confidence the weapons are being "employed judiciously."

The ceasefire extension Trump announced earlier this week has opened a window for the U.S. to replenish assets in the Middle East, but defense planners draw a sharp distinction between capability and capacity. While U.S. systems remain technically superior, the ability to replace expended weapons quickly is limited, and air defense interceptors are in simultaneous demand across the Middle East, Europe, and the Indo-Pacific.

The CSIS analysis noted the immediate risk to ongoing operations is manageable, but warned that the deeper danger "will persist for many years" and concerns future conflicts — particularly a potential confrontation with China in the Pacific, where Indo-Pacific Command relies heavily on the same finite stockpiles.

stories.headline: ESPN's Stephen A. Smith Feuds With WWE's Danhausen as Knicks Trail Hawks 2-1
stories.summary:
WWE personality Danhausen and ESPN broadcaster Stephen A. Smith have exchanged barbs on social media and television after Danhausen appeared on ESPN's 'First Take' last week and issued a mock 'curse' on Smith, a vocal New York Knicks fan. The Knicks have since fallen behind the Atlanta Hawks 2-1 in their first-round NBA playoff series, which Danhausen's supporters have attributed to the curse.

Smith fired back on social media, refusing to apologize and laying responsibility squarely on the Knicks organization rather than supernatural forces, saying 'heads need to roll' — including the team's president, general manager, coach, and players — if New York fails to advance. Danhausen escalated the feud on Friday Night SmackDown by carrying a large photo of Smith's face into the arena.

The rivalry, while lighthearted, has drawn attention given the Knicks' unexpected struggles against a young Hawks team. Game 4 of the series is scheduled for Saturday evening at 6 p.m. ET.

stories.headline: Fox News Poll Shows Democrats Leading GOP on Economy for First Time as Trump Approval Hits Second-Term Low
stories.summary:
President Trump's approval rating has fallen to its lowest point of his second term, with a new Fox News poll showing Democrats leading Republicans by four percentage points on the economy — the first time the GOP has trailed on that metric in recent memory, according to Axios and The Hill. The decline comes as Trump's major policy bets, including sweeping tariffs, military engagement in Iran, and aggressive congressional redistricting efforts, face mounting public skepticism.

Analysts note that Trump, as a term-limited president, is insulated from personal electoral consequences but that his party is not. Republican lawmakers facing 2026 midterm races are increasingly exposed to the fallout from policies they backed at the administration's urging. Redistricting efforts promoted by the White House have drawn particular criticism after some GOP members expressed concern that the strategy could backfire in competitive districts.

The economic approval gap is seen as especially significant because Republicans have long held a structural advantage on fiscal and economic issues. The shift comes amid public concerns about tariff-driven price increases and uncertainty over the administration's trade posture.

The White House has not publicly responded to the Fox News poll figures. Administration allies argue that short-term polling volatility is not predictive of midterm outcomes and that the economic fundamentals — including employment levels — remain a strong baseline for Republican candidates to run on.

stories.headline: Update: Key Suspect in Syria's 2013 Tadamon Massacre Arrested in Hama Province
stories.summary:
Syrian authorities have arrested Amjad Youssef, identified by the interior ministry as the primary perpetrator of the April 2013 Tadamon massacre in Damascus, in which soldiers executed bound and blindfolded civilians by shooting them into a pit. Interior Minister Anas Khattab announced the arrest, describing it as the result of a "well-executed" security operation. Footage from the Syrian Observatory for Human Rights appeared to show Youssef in custody, his face bloodied, in a police vehicle in Hama province.

The Tadamon killings gained wide international attention in 2022 when video filmed by the perpetrators themselves was leaked, showing soldiers leading victims one by one to a mass grave before shooting them. Human Rights Watch documented evidence of 288 deaths in Tadamon overall, with 41 killed in the specific April 2013 incident captured on video. HRW described the site as "a huge crime scene" and called the footage evidence of apparent war crimes by the Assad government.

The arrest marks one of the most significant judicial accountability steps since the fall of Bashar al-Assad's government in December 2024, when rebel forces toppled his administration after more than 13 years of civil war that killed over half a million people. Syria's new governing authorities have signaled intent to pursue figures implicated in atrocities committed by the former regime.

Human rights organizations have welcomed the arrest while cautioning that accountability efforts must extend broadly. HRW senior researcher Hiba Zayadin described the leaked video as evidence of the former government's "callous disregard for people's lives" and called the Tadamon massacre one incident in a larger pattern of state violence.

stories.headline: Update: Arrest Made in Mall of Louisiana Shooting; One Suspect Still at Large
stories.summary:
A 17-year-old suspect, Markel Lee, turned himself in to Baton Rouge police Friday in connection with a shooting at the Mall of Louisiana. Lee was charged with first-degree murder, five counts of attempted first-degree murder, and illegal use of a weapon. A 43-year-old man injured in the shooting remains hospitalized in critical condition, according to Baton Rouge Police Chief T.J. Morse.

Authorities said a second suspect remains at large. The shooting drew significant law enforcement attention and prompted a large police response at one of Louisiana's major retail destinations. Officials have not yet disclosed the circumstances or motive behind the attack.

The arrest comes as investigators continue to piece together the sequence of events. The case has attracted coverage from multiple national outlets as authorities work to bring all parties involved to justice.

stories.headline: Israeli Police Under Investigation After Officers Reportedly Cut Palestinian Flag from Jewish Skullcap
stories.summary:
British-Israeli academic Alex Sinclair, 53, was detained by Israeli police near his home in Modiin, central Israel, on Monday after a complaint was filed about his Jewish kippah, which bore both an Israeli and a Palestinian flag. Sinclair said he was held in a cell and when his kippah was returned, the portion displaying the Palestinian flag had been cut off. The incident has attracted domestic and international attention after Sinclair shared the account on social media.

Israeli police confirmed in a statement that officers responded to a hotline complaint and brought the individual to a police station for 'clarification,' after which he was released. The force said a complaint has been filed with its internal investigations division and declined to provide further details. There is no explicit Israeli law banning public display of the Palestinian flag, though courts have allowed police to remove such symbols if deemed a threat to public order.

The case highlights tensions surrounding far-right National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir's standing instruction to police to clamp down on Palestinian flag displays, a policy Israeli rights groups have described as legally questionable. Critics argue the incident illustrates a chilling effect on free expression within Israel, while supporters of the crackdown contend the flag can be associated with designated terrorist organizations. Sinclair, who had worn the kippah without incident for roughly two decades, called the episode 'surreal.'

stories.headline: Everest Flood Warning System Left to Rust, Leaving Thousands at Risk
stories.summary:
A UN-supported early warning system designed to protect thousands of people living downstream of the Imja glacial lake in Nepal's Everest region may no longer be functional, Nepalese officials have admitted to the BBC. Siren towers have been left to rust for years with no maintenance, some have had their batteries stolen, and satellite data transmissions relaying the lake's water level have been unreliable. No inspection of the $3.5 million system has been carried out since the lake was partially drained in 2016.

Local Sherpa community leaders told the BBC they have repeatedly traveled to Kathmandu to petition Nepal's Department of Hydrology and Meteorology for repairs, without result. Scientists warn that accelerating glacier melt driven by climate change is causing Himalayan glacial lakes to expand dangerously, raising the risk of sudden and catastrophic outburst floods. Ice loss rates in the Hindu Khush Himalaya region have doubled since 2000, according to the Kathmandu-based International Centre for Integrated Mountain Development.

The Imja lake sits at more than 5,000 meters above sea level, and a burst could sweep away villages, trekking routes, and bridges. Spring is peak climbing and trekking season, meaning the population at risk currently includes not only permanent residents but large numbers of visitors to the region. Officials have not announced plans for emergency repairs.

stories.headline: Palantir CEO's 'Anti-Woke' Manifesto Goes Viral as Firm Holds Major NHS and UK Defence Contracts
stories.summary:
A 22-point social media post by Palantir co-founder and CEO Alex Karp has surpassed 30 million views on X, drawing renewed scrutiny to the data analytics company and its expanding role in British and American public institutions. Karp criticized the view that all cultures are equal, backed AI-enabled weapons systems, called postwar disarmament of Germany and Japan an 'overcorrection,' and condemned what he described as 'ruthless exposure' of the private lives of public figures. The post is drawn from a 2025 book co-authored by Karp titled 'The Technological Republic.'

Palantir holds a £300 million contract to build a data platform for the NHS, a £240 million Ministry of Defence contract for AI-enabled targeting technology described as supporting the 'kill-chain,' and deals with the Financial Conduct Authority and 11 UK police forces. The company employs approximately 950 people in the UK, about 17 percent of its global workforce, and has multimillion-dollar contracts with the U.S. government and NATO allies, including use of its systems in the conflict with Iran.

Critics, including academic ethicists and the British Medical Association, have raised concerns about the concentration of sensitive data — including NHS patient records — within a firm whose leadership holds controversial political views. Palantir insiders describe the company's role as 'plumbing,' connecting incompatible data systems for analysis. Supporters argue the firm is uniquely equipped to manage complex data challenges, while opponents cite its work with U.S. immigration enforcement and Israel's military as disqualifying factors.

stories.headline: LeBron James Forces Overtime with Late Three-Pointer as Lakers Take 3-0 Series Lead Over Rockets
stories.summary:
LeBron James scored 29 points, including a tying three-pointer with 13 seconds remaining in regulation, to rally the Los Angeles Lakers from a six-point deficit in the final 30 seconds and force overtime in a 112-108 win over the Houston Rockets on Friday night. The victory gives the Lakers a 3-0 lead in the Western Conference first-round playoff series, leaving them one win from a sweep. Game 4 is scheduled for Sunday in Houston.

Marcus Smart contributed 21 points, 10 assists, and key overtime plays, while Rui Hachimura added 22 points. The Lakers played without injured starters Luka Dončić and Austin Reaves, placing additional burden on the 41-year-old James, who logged more than 45 minutes. Houston played without Kevin Durant for the second consecutive game — this time due to a sprained ankle — and the Rockets' inability to close out games in the fourth quarter proved decisive again.

Alperen Sengun led Houston with 33 points and 16 rebounds, and Amen Thompson added 26 points and 11 rebounds. Rockets coach Ime Udoka acknowledged a recurring problem with late-game execution, calling certain mistakes 'egregious.' James also recorded an assist on an alley-oop to his son Bronny earlier in the game.

stories.headline: DOJ Moves to Reclassify Marijuana as Less Dangerous Drug in Major Policy Shift
stories.summary:
The Department of Justice announced it would immediately loosen restrictions on certain marijuana products and move to reclassify cannabis as a less dangerous controlled substance, representing one of the most significant changes to federal drug policy in decades. The move would affect the $47 billion legal marijuana industry, which currently operates in a legal gray zone where state-licensed businesses cannot access standard federal banking services or tax deductions due to the drug's Schedule I classification.

Rescheduling cannabis to a lower schedule would not fully legalize it at the federal level but would reduce criminal penalties and regulatory burdens associated with the drug's current status. Proponents of reclassification argue it aligns federal policy more closely with scientific consensus and the reality of widespread state-level legalization. Critics, including some law enforcement groups and social conservatives, have historically argued that loosening federal restrictions sends the wrong message and could increase youth use.

The announcement had been anticipated following years of advocacy from the cannabis industry, public health researchers, and bipartisan lawmakers. The formal rulemaking process is expected to take additional steps before reclassification is finalized.

stories.headline: Armed Groups Launch Coordinated Attacks Across Mali, Including Near Capital
stories.summary:
Armed groups launched coordinated attacks across Mali early Saturday, including near the capital Bamako, the country's military confirmed. The army said fighting was ongoing and that defense and security forces were engaged in repelling attackers. Witnesses reported explosions and sustained gunfire near the Kati military base, a major installation outside Bamako, with soldiers deployed to block roads in the area.

Attacks were also reported in Gao in the east and Sevare in central Mali. Flights into Bamako were cancelled, though it remained unclear whether the airport itself had been targeted. The U.S. Embassy in Mali issued a shelter-in-place advisory for American citizens, citing explosions and gunfire near Modibo Keita International Airport and the Kati base.

No group has claimed responsibility. Mali has been governed by a military junta since a 2020 coup led by Gen. Assimi Goïta, who came to power promising to restore security but has presided over a continuing jihadist insurgency. The junta expelled French forces and a UN peacekeeping mission and replaced them with Russian mercenaries, yet large portions of northern and eastern Mali remain outside government control.

stories.headline: Update: U.S. Sanctions Major Chinese Refinery and 40 Shippers Over Iranian Oil Purchases
stories.summary:
The Trump administration announced sweeping new sanctions Friday targeting a major Chinese oil refinery and roughly 40 shipping companies and tankers involved in transporting Iranian crude. The measures, first reported by the Associated Press, target Hengli Petrochemical's facility in Dalian — one of China's largest independent refineries with a processing capacity of about 400,000 barrels per day — which the Treasury Department says has received Iranian crude since 2023, generating hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue for the Iranian military.

The sanctions cut off the named companies from the U.S. financial system and penalize anyone who does business with them. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the administration would continue to "constrict the network of vessels, intermediaries and buyers Iran relies on to move its oil to global markets." Earlier this month, Treasury sent warning letters to financial institutions in China, Hong Kong, the UAE and Oman threatening secondary sanctions for dealings with Iran.

The move represents a significant escalation in the U.S. campaign to choke off Iran's primary revenue source, and comes as the U.S. has also imposed a physical blockade on the Strait of Hormuz. The timing is notable: President Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping are due to meet in China within weeks, raising questions about how Beijing will respond. China purchases 80 to 90 percent of Iran's oil exports, much of it transported through a shadow fleet with obscured origins.

stories.headline: Update: OpenAI's Sam Altman Apologizes for Failing to Alert Police Before Canada School Shooting
stories.summary:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman issued a formal apology Friday after it emerged his company failed to notify law enforcement about the online behavior of an 18-year-old who killed eight people — including five children and an educator — at a school in Tumbler Ridge, British Columbia in February. In a letter posted on B.C. Premier David Eby's social media, Altman said he was "deeply sorry" the company did not alert police to the account, which had been banned in June for "furtherance of violent activities."

OpenAI had previously disclosed that it identified the shooter's account through internal abuse-detection systems but determined at the time that the activity did not meet its threshold for referral to law enforcement. The company banned the account months before the attack. Altman said he had spoken directly with Tumbler Ridge's mayor and the B.C. premier, who conveyed the anger and grief of the community.

Premier Eby accepted the apology but called it "necessary, and yet grossly insufficient for the devastation done to the families of Tumbler Ridge." The case raises urgent questions about the obligations of AI companies when their systems detect potential threats to public safety, and whether self-imposed thresholds for law enforcement referrals are adequate. Altman pledged to work with governments to help prevent similar tragedies.

stories.headline: Update: Soldier's Prediction Market Bet on Maduro Raid Sparks Push to Regulate Markets for National Security Risks
stories.summary:
The arrest of a U.S. Army Special Forces soldier charged with using classified information about the raid to capture Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro to place a winning Polymarket bet is intensifying congressional scrutiny of prediction markets. The case, announced by the Justice Department Thursday, marks the first time the Commodity Futures Trading Commission has pursued insider trading charges tied to a prediction market, setting a legal precedent with broad implications for the fast-growing industry.

In response, Sen. Bernie Moreno (R-Ohio) introduced a resolution Friday that would amend Senate rules to ban members of Congress from participating in prediction markets, citing concerns about potential insider trading on platforms that wager on political and geopolitical events. Lawmakers from both parties have raised concerns in recent months about the ethical and national security risks posed by the markets, which have grown significantly in popularity.

The case underscores a tension that has built since prediction markets gained mainstream traction: their real-money incentives to gather information could, in theory, encourage participants to exploit privileged access. Critics argue the markets need enforceable regulations analogous to securities law, while proponents say they aggregate useful public information and that this case represents an extreme outlier rather than a systemic problem.

stories.headline: Palestinians Vote in First Gaza Election Since 2006 as West Bank Polls Also Open
stories.summary:
Palestinians went to the polls Saturday in local elections spanning the occupied West Bank and, for the first time since 2006, a part of Gaza — specifically Deir al-Balah in central Gaza, where 12 polling stations opened for roughly 70,000 eligible voters. More than one million Palestinians across the territories were eligible to participate, according to the Ramallah-based Central Elections Commission.

Hamas was barred from standing in Deir al-Balah after refusing to recognize the authority of the Palestinian Liberation Organisation, a condition required of all candidates. Several other factions boycotted the elections on the same grounds, leaving Fatah — the faction led by Palestinian Authority President Mahmoud Abbas — as the dominant force on ballots across both the West Bank and Gaza. Reuters reported that one slate of Deir al-Balah candidates was widely viewed as Hamas-aligned despite the group's formal exclusion.

The elections reflect the enduring rift between Hamas and Fatah following Hamas's 2007 seizure of Gaza after winning the last elections there. Hamas's popularity has reportedly fallen in Gaza due to the devastation of the recent war, while support for it has risen in the West Bank amid widespread disillusionment with the PA, which many Palestinians view as corrupt and ineffective. Results are expected late Saturday or Sunday.

stories.headline: 31 Sloths Die Before Opening of Florida Wildlife Attraction, State Report Finds
stories.summary:
Thirty-one sloths imported for a new Orlando attraction called Sloth World died before the exhibit's planned spring opening, according to a report released Friday by the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission. The report details two separate incidents: 21 sloths from Guyana died in December 2024 after being housed in a Florida warehouse without adequate heat, and 10 sloths from Peru arrived in February 2025, with two dead on arrival and the remaining eight dying from poor health shortly thereafter.

Investigators found that the warehouse lacked water or electricity when the animals arrived. Space heaters were purchased to warm the facility, but the heaters tripped a fuse and shut off overnight, leaving the tropical animals — which require temperatures between 70°F and 86°F — exposed to temperatures that dropped as low as 46°F the following week. The attraction's listed vice president, Peter Bandre, acknowledged to investigators that it was "too late to cancel the shipment."

Sloth World owner Ben Agresta disputed the findings, telling a local Fox affiliate that the animals died from an undetectable virus and that there is "so much false and inaccurate information out there right now." The FWC closed its investigations without issuing a written warning or citation, though a spokesperson confirmed the inquiry is complete. The case has drawn attention to oversight of exotic animal importation and the welfare standards required of commercial wildlife attractions.
